moveAnnotations.annotation: We begin with the Nimzo-Larsen Attack, an unusual but solid opening that prepares to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2 while controlling the important central squares from the flanks. Rather than fighting for the center with pawns immediately, we're taking a flexible, positional approach—we'll support the center with pieces and control key diagonals. This move is less common than 1.e4 or 1.d4, which gives us the practical advantage of avoiding well-prepared opponents and entering rich, strategically complex positions.

Black claims the center immediately, which is the classical response to our flank opening. This move supports central control and opens lines for Black's bishop and queen, but it also commits Black's pawn structure early—we'll exploit this by building pressure against e5 with our pieces and the long diagonal.

We complete our fianchetto setup, and now our bishop aims powerfully down the long diagonal toward e5 and the center. The Bb2 controls crucial squares (a1-h8 diagonal) and puts immediate pressure on Black's central pawn; combined with our setup, this bishop will be one of our most active pieces, attacking e5 and supporting our eventual central play.

Black develops naturally, defending the e5 pawn and preparing to develop the kingside. The knight on c6 is a solid developing move that supports Black's center, but now Black must decide how to handle the tension—we're building pressure that will soon demand a response.

This is the Pachman Gambit, where we attack Black's e5 pawn immediately and offer dynamic counterplay for any material Black takes. By playing f4, we're sacrificing a pawn to open lines (especially the f-file and diagonals) and create immediate tactical threats; if Black captures exf4, we recapture with tempo while keeping strong central control and piece activity. This aggressive follow-up transforms our positional setup into a sharp, concrete middlegame where our bishop pair and open lines compensate for the pawn.
